body:
I've realized that this change would cause a lot of developers issues if it was implemented like this: everyone relying on `Sprite` would suddenly have the screen-space (i.e. the final rendered size) of their `Sprite`s be different and they'd need to fix it by changing the scale.

Basically it would be a breaking change in the sense that any developer that upgrades to a new version of Three which includes this change would suddenly have their sprites be way smaller than before the upgrade, without a clear reason why. Existing apps would suddenly look different.

So I'll make this be dependent on another parameter on `SpriteMaterial`. This way, it's an opt-in change, and existing applications that upgrade to newer versions of Three won't be surprised.
author: alberto-taiuti-skydio

body:
How about a one-liner in your app, instead, applied whenever `camera.fov` is changed?

For example,

```js
spriteA.scale.setScalar( 0.2 * camera.fov / 60 );
```
author: WestLangley

body:
@WestLangley 

> How about a one-liner in your app, instead, applied whenever camera.fov is changed?

That's the first thing I went for, but then I realized that:

1. Since one potentially wants to animate changes to the FOV, by changing the scale of each sprite in a scene one by one at 60FPS is slower than just using the data already available in the vertex shader, especially in the case where there are a lot of sprites in a scene
2. Especially when you have a large number of sprites in a scene, like in our app, we can save CPU cycles by just reusing what's already available in the vertex shader
3. Since we use react-three-fiber, to do the above in it you'd have to subscribe to render loop updates using `useFrame`. Which means that for every `Sprite` in the scene you are creating a subscription that needs to be called and code to be run. When the number of sprites is large, this eats up CPU cycles

So in general the reason to do this is that we can achieve the same as the one liner above but do it without using any extra CPU cycles nor having to remember to do it for each sprite.
author: alberto-taiuti-skydio

body: @WestLangley what do you think of the above? Could we maybe try and work on this so we can merge it? Happy to discuss the changes if needed!
author: alberto-taiuti-skydio

body:
Actually, I am not the one who makes that decision. :-)

@Mugen87 will make a decision based on what he believes is best for the project.
author: WestLangley

body:
> Especially when you have a large number of sprites in a scene, like in our app, we can save CPU cycles by just reusing what's already available in the vertex shader

When you have a large number of sprites (let's say > 100), `THREE.Sprite` is problematic in any event because of the resulting number of draw calls. This is a way larger overhead than animating the scale property for all sprites every frame. So  using instanced planes with `InstancedMesh` or even `THREE.Points` would be a noticeably faster alternative than `THREE.Sprite` in these scenarios (assuming materials can be shared to a certain degree). When I remember correctly, a plane can be oriented towards the camera by doing this:
```js
plane.quaternion.copy( camera.quaternion );
```
Using this in combination with `InstancedMesh` you have instanced billboards.

The workaround @WestLangley has shared seems like a good solution. Considering the changes to the core (a new material property and the shader/renderer modifications), I'm afraid the PR seems not justified at the moment.

body:
Hi, yes sure 

- happened in Needle Engine where we had two GltfLoaderPlugins without a name and didn't notice that one of them wasnt being called anymore, causing bugs / breaking functionality in some cases
- without a name the plugin gets `undefined` as a key and whichever comes last just overrides the previously registered one in the gltfloader plugins dict and only this last one will then receive callbacks. The others would just silently stop working. So this should help to find those mistakes early

body: This is OK with me, though I would prefer to just log the error message and not to print the implementation of the plugin.

body:
The idea with logging the implementation was to get an idea which plugin was misbehaving

author: marwie

body:
I'm hoping it is enough to basically say, a plugin was invalid because it has no name, and let the developer set a breakpoint. Devtools will point you to the source line of the log. 

Slightly subjective opinion — but I prefer that libraries log strings and not objects, since they may run in remote environments with logging capabilities that differ from the browser's, and I do know of places GLTFLoader is used like that.

body:
Thanks for filing the PR!

The project wants to focus geometry generators on the most relevant use cases in order to keep the modules manageable. When PRs like this are getting submitted, we have to decide on a case-by-case basis whether the feature addition is appropriate or not. 

At least to me, the changes of this PR are too use case specific. Besides, reordering the data is also something that you could implement on app level. Against this backdrop, I prefer to not merge this PR.
author: Mugen87

body:
Unlike `WebGLRenderer.render()`, `PMREMGenerator.fromScene()` expects an instance of `Scene`. That's because the internal `_sceneToCubeUV()` method sets the `background` property at the end of its implementation.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/a0cb76952dae8af9699ec85b66e79a4a59c52ee8/src/extras/PMREMGenerator.js#L379

In the example, the `sky` object ends up with an `undefined` background property. Nothing breaks because of this but after all it is not ideal.

However, I don't think the PR is correct in its current form since the scene you are using is the same scene that holds the water and box. It's more correct to use a second scene, add the sky to it, execute `PMREMGenerator.fromScene()` and then add the sky back to its original scene. Or alternatively, you just use one scene but set the `visible` property of the water and box to `false` before `fromScene()` runs (and then restore the values).

> Do you mind removing the build files from your PR?

@Mugen87 Sure thing. Though I think removing the build files will cause the e2e test for `examples/webgl_loader_texture_dds.html` to start failing again. Without the build files, the test was just drawing solid black instead of the expected image. After looking into it, I found that this was happening because without the build files, the following error was being thrown:

```
SyntaxError: The requested module 'three' does not provide an export named 'RGB_BPTC_SIGNED_Format'
```
author: CoryG89

body: The E2E test automatically perform a build on the latest `src` so there should be no need to update build files.
author: Mugen87

body:
The problem is that `RenderPass` now always overwrites whatever clear color (and alpha) is configured by the renderer which is not right.

The parameter `clearColor` not only controls the default value of the render pass's clear color but also whether or not the existing clear color and alpha values of the renderer are overwritten. This behavior needs to be retained.
author: Mugen87

body:
> The problem is now that RenderPass always overwrites whatever clear color (and alpha) is configured by the renderer which is not right. The parameter clearColor not only controls the default value of the clear color but also whether or not the existing clear color and alpha values are overwritten. This behavior needs to be retained.

In other words, the correct default values for clear color and alpha must be the current clear color and alpha values of the renderer. 

I admit that the current behavior of how clear alpha works is a bit confusing. But I think we need an update in `render()` to fix this.

body:
@sunag I would like to ask a simple question, how do you locate or analyze this problem, I can not find any helpful information in the error message of the debugging panel, I tried to debug, but it did not work well. Maybe I'm not familiar with the `WebGPURenderer` module code.

I wonder if it is convenient for you to share your experience.
author: linbingquan

body:
The error presented was from a WGSL generated a `vec4f( vec3f(), vec3f() )`, so I figured it was a compilation problem, TSL automatically converts the type differences, and I started to investigate if there was a specific Node with problem that `AxesHelper` was being used, then I noticed the Vertex Color, but the code was correct, it would certainly be a Node System issue.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/52c85845a1dcb276fec29a4315f5d04aad6d00d3/examples/jsm/nodes/materials/NodeMaterial.js#L164

This was a global fix for Node System, it will certainly appear at some point or another.

When `construct()` returns a Node, some Nodes were using `.getNodeType()` to get the type of a child Node, and the data was not getting updated with the return of `.construct()`. I arrived at this conclusion debugging the line above, in the `construct()` and `generate()` processes.

I'm hopeful that after stabilizing the Node System we will create documentation explaining these processes better, mainly so that users can create their own Nodes or import from other 3d software.

body:
To summarize a few things happening in this PR and its demo —

1. When the _browser_ supports Display P3 (In 2023: Chrome and Safari), and the developer knows their scene contains wide gamut content, we assign `ColorManagement.workingColorSpace = LinearDisplayP3ColorSpace` to enable wide gamut _rendering_.
2. When the _display_ supports Display P3 (In 2023: Most macOS, iOS, and Pixel devices) we assign `renderer.outputColorSpace = DisplayP3ColorSpace` to enable wide gamut _output_. This may change during a session, if displays are (dis)connected or windows are moved to another display.
3. When a texture's `.colorSpace` property indicates that it uses primaries differing from those of the working color space (e.g. sRGB textures in a Linear Display P3 working color space) the renderer enables `gl.UNPACK_COLORSPACE_CONVERSION_WEBGL` to gamut map the texture to the working color space. This operation requires that the texture's ICC profile be correct; setting `texture.colorSpace` alone is not enough. WebGL cannot do this for compressed textures or data textures — they must already use the working primaries.
4. When a texture's `.colorSpace` property indicates that it uses a supported transfer function (both sRGB and Display P3 use the "sRGB" transfer function) the renderer chooses an internal format such that WebGL will automatically apply the sRGB EOTF when sampling from the texture.
5. After rendering, pixels are encoded to the output color space.

As a result of (3) and (4), color images are typically uploaded to WebGL in either sRGB or Display P3 (whichever is the non-linear analog to the linear working color space). When the GPU samples the textures, it decodes each sample by applying the sRGB EOTF, to obtain a sample in the working color space (Linear sRGB or Linear Display P3).

author: hybridherbst

body:
Sorry for the late feedback but the whole camera scaling topic is a bit complicated.

So far `three.js` stated cameras should not be scaled because of unexpected/undefined behavior. Personally, I could not imagine use cases where someone wants to scale a camera. But now we have seen glTF asset that might contain a scaling (see #24800) and WebXR use cases.

Unfortunately, we can not properly review this PR until we have a clear policy about processing scaled cameras. For example, the glTF spec says about the view matrix (https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#view-matrix):

> The view matrix is derived from the global transform of the node containing the camera with the scaling ignored.

We currently don't do that but maybe we should. Even if this means we need different approaches for fixing WebXR use cases. I suggest we close this PR until it's clear how we want to move on. This should be discussed in #26659.

author: github-actions

body: /ping @sciecode
author: Mugen87

body:
Thank you for the ping Mugen87.

While vertical strip cubemaps aren't uncommon, even Unity allows for different [cubemap](https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/class-Cubemap.html) layouts. This PR enforces this set configuration for all uses of cubemap RenderTargets - which maybe not be what other users want.

I considered this situation back when I first implemented the exporter, but decided it was best if users themselves handled how cubemaps should be flatten; by constructing the final RenderTarget in user-space.
 
If there's sufficient demand, perhaps we could implement a dedicated API to handle some of the cases. But I don't believe this proposed solution is universal enough in it's current implementation.
author: sciecode

body:
> The exporter currently crashes because [no face index is specified when calling `renderer.readRenderTargetPixels(...)`](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/jsm/exporters/EXRExporter.js#L128)

However we should inform the user of this limitation when trying to read from a cubeTexture, it shouldn't just crash on them.
author: sciecode

body:
> This PR enforces this set configuration for all uses of cubemap RenderTargets - which maybe not be what other users want.

I do agree. So for the moment a simple error with explicit message about cubeTexture may be enough.

However, i'm wondering if the EXRExporter could accept any textures, or buffers, instead of a render target as an input. 

@sciecode 
I'm making a tool to export environment textures, I would like to provide the common cubemap layout (equirectangular, cross, strip), and I only need from the EXRExporter the final steps (compression, file encoding), so what about another public method? Does it make sense?

Conversely, I think now that the best way to export cubemaps is to use a dedicated render target used in conjonction with a dedicated shader that will encore the pixels according to the different existing standard layout. 
@Mugen87 
Do you believe that an example for cubemap encoding / exporting is something that threejs may desire to add to its existing examples? (If so I may begin by a threejs style example).
author: jniac

body:
> Do you believe that an example for cubemap encoding / exporting is something that threejs may desire to add to its existing examples? (If so I may begin by a threejs style example).

It would be good to have an example for each type of exporter so adding `misc_exporter_exr` sounds fine. The example could use `lil-gui` to provide a simple dropdown menu for exporting different kind of texture setups. This would be the ideal place for adding a cube map example.
author: Mugen87

body:
> However, i'm wondering if the EXRExporter could accept any textures, or buffers, instead of a render target as an input.

Yes, I do believe so. Specifically a `DataTexture` interface would make sense to me, cause all information required by exporter is provided by default (ImageBuffer, Width/Height, Format, Type). Having the current `.parse` API alongside this proposed `.parseDataTexture`, would be my preferred way of moving forward.

Would you like to work on this PR @jniac, or you'd prefer if I did it?
author: sciecode

body:
I can do it myself, following the same style you used (very simple class methods calling pure functions with explicit names), then you'll check if it's ok for you. I'm wondering what could be the name of the new function. 

If `parse()` is for RTT, should we propose `parseTexture()`  for textures ? 

This will even allows a `parseCubeTexture()` in the future which may return an array of 6 exr files (instead of a single strip file).

Should we rename `parse()` to `parseRenderTarget()`, keep the old name and mark it as deprecated?
author: jniac

body:
> I can do it myself, following the same style you used (very simple class methods calling pure functions with explicit names), then you'll check if it's ok for you.

Sure, feel free to ping and I'll review the changes.

I'm famously bad at naming APIs 😆 So perhaps @Mugen87 and others could help us with that.

My only concern with `parseTexture()` is that it implies any texture can be exported, but at this moment only `DataTexture` will be supported. 

The name `parse` was also used based on the API provided by other exporters, which never made much sense to me - in this context. If we are to change, perhaps it would makee sense to also reconsider that.

I'm not particular about any of this, we should just try to minimize changes as to avoid confusion by users, but whatever others agree on, is fine with me.
author: sciecode

body:
Let's avoid to question the `parse*()` syntax for now since I fear this will only defer or even block the PR. 

I would prefer to keep `EXRExporter` similar to `KTX2Exporter`. When using render targets, `KTX2Exporter` provides the following API:
```js
exporter.parse( renderer, renderTarget );
```
However, the exporter also supports textures like so:
```js
exporter.parse( texture );
```
I would do the same for `EXRExporter`. Meaning you check the argument types in `parse()` and then execute different code paths.
author: Mugen87

body: Now that #26810 has been merged (🎉 ), how should we proceed with this PR?
author: Mugen87

body:
> Now that #26810 has been merged (🎉 ), how should we proceed with this PR?

We can close it I think. [I made an example for exporting cube texture into various formats (equirectangular + horizontal stripe for now)](https://jniac.github.io/three.js/examples/misc_exporting_environment_texture.html) but it's a separate topic. So we can close this one.

Could you tell me if it is worth integrating such an example? If so, i can complete the example, clean the code and create a new PR.
author: jniac

body:
> So we can close this one.

Okay!

> Could you tell me if it is worth integrating such an example? If so, i can complete the example, clean the code and create a new PR.

I'm not very familiar with the EXR standard so I'm not sure how to proceed with cube map conventions. But I admit it could be useful for other devs to see how stripe or cross layouts are created. I'm okay with whatever @sciecode thinks is best.
author: Mugen87

body:
OpenEXR spec defines an [envmap](https://openexr.com/en/latest/ReadingAndWritingImageFiles.html#environment-maps) hint attribute for equirectangular & vertical-stripe envmaps, however this attribute is mostly ignored by most loaders, and completely stripped out by all editing software that I've used. So, it should really be up to user which cubemap convention they want, as the file will be treated as plain data in almost every case.

> Could you tell me if it is worth integrating such an example? If so, i can complete the example, clean the code and create a new PR.

Since we expect the user to generate their own preferred data-layout, showcasing how to generate different layouts could indeed be useful. I think we should just add cross layout to your example, as Mugen suggested.

author: OndrejSpanel

body:
Here is the issue repro: https://jsfiddle.net/OndrejSpanel/531fa2e8/116/

Steps:

1. Render cyan sphere, writing 4 to stencil
  ![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/6927223/242067f7-f386-45fa-8913-5963e014370d)

2. Render green sphere with a stencil test value == 4, setting the write mask to zero (any other render call would do, just set the write mask so that bit 4 is not cleared later):
  ![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/6927223/4d1ab728-a057-4b7d-b6f5-d2958ef99cd4)

3. Clear the backbuffer---- here the issue happens. The stencil is not cleared.

4. Render the green sphere again with a stencil test value == 4 on a freshly cleared backbuffer:
  ![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/6927223/4fe99c72-dcd5-42d3-8e2a-6a4dc94a6a83)
  Nothing should appear, as the stencil buffer should be zero after clear. It is however not cleared and the same shape as in step 2 appears.

body:
>  This was also probably the reason that the transformGroup property was created in the first place.

`transformGroup` was added so you can select multiple objects and drag them as a group.

The [official example](https://threejs.org/examples/misc_controls_drag) demonstrates the normal dragging mode and the group feature. Do you mind sharing a live example that shows the use case that currently does not work? That would make it easier to see what the PR fixes.
author: Mugen87

body:
I've created a live example by myself to better understand the PR (the blue box is a child of the red one): https://jsfiddle.net/dvhbrt4y/3/

TBH, I find it a bit confusing that descendant objects are not draggable with `recursive` setting to `false`. I understand there are use cases where this behavior might be wanted but still it seems quite specific to me.

Do you mind explaining your use case a bit so it becomes more clear why you need this behavior?
author: Mugen87

body:
Sorry, I've been so busy in the past week that I haven't been able to create an example. In regards to my use case, I have a factory machine which is composed of parts. These parts can rotate independently of the machine, which is why I haven't merged everything into one object. I want to be able to move this machine around, but it makes less sense to be able to move the rotating parts independently of the machine.
I hijacked your fiddle [here](https://jsfiddle.net/ssk4988/wufc0r8a/16/) to show such a use case.
I didn't animate it properly, but it gives you an idea of why I wouldn't want children to be independently draggable.
Thanks.
author: ssk4988

body: I see. The fiddle indeed helped to understand the issue. The default behavior isn't an option here and `transformGroup` isn't the right feature as well. Since the new property does not affect the existing usage of `DragControls`, I'm fine with the addition.

body:
Yes very, this is my topic:

https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&source=web&rct=j&opi=89978449&url=https://tore.tuhh.de/bitstream/11420/1439/1/GPGPU_FFT_Ocean_Simulation.pdf&ved=2ahUKEwj07qP5vbWBAxUx1AIHHdE9AO4QFnoECBgQAQ&usg=AOvVaw0_z-QXC4vcYeIYLsP5WBfM

With r156 I also created the butterfly texture (pages 26 and 40) and the init spectras. This works very well with the node system and wgslFn. For the butterfly texture I only had to write my own bit-reverse fn but these are minor obstacles. The textures look exactly like in the paper. The only thing I was missing was the textureStore functionality before r156. I could have done the pingpong mechanic thing more complicatedly with two separate shaders, but with the pingpong possibility it's cleaner. I have this IFFT ocean since may in WebGL2, but with lots of render targets. But now I want to do all of this with computer shaders. Phil Crowther inspired me to do this because he has been looking for a gpu IFFT ocean for a longer time. Once I've finished it, he can add it as a module in three.js.
Thanks to your comment with the textureNode and the uniforms, I now understand the node system quite well and I like it.
author: Spiri0

body:
Is this how `webgpu_compute_texture_pingpong` is supposed to look like?

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/97088/a580fe99-1381-4600-aa40-3ef2c1b4cc40
author: mrdoob

body:
This looks good. It looks similar to what I have already implemented in WebGL2 with renderTargets. You probably used the noise pattern that I saw in the r157 example, but that's a user matter. The mechanism certainly looks correct. To us it just looks like noise. However, for the Fourier mechanism, these are numerous different wavelengths that evolve over time. The thing with the StorageTexture was a great idea. I wonder if you or sunag are aware that you have essentially made the DataTexture, which generates the textures on the CPU side, superfluous with the StorageTexture. In any case, I only see advantages in the StorageTexture. 
Being able to generate textures on gpu side with the compute shaders is an enormous milestone.
author: Spiri0

body: Improved the example to make it more visually appealing in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/commit/d6c8dd0b50a2994c05f94687ed0dfa44ae61ee4c
author: mrdoob

body: The example turned out very well. Not because it happens to be what I need, but because it conveys the node system very well in conjunction with slightly more extensive but not too large WGSL shaders. It also teaches pretty much everything one need to use compute shaders with their full potential for texture generation. In my opinion, the 3 compute examples and the materials example are sufficient to learn nearly everything about the node system. I'm now really looking forward to the weekend to port my IFFT generator with r157 into my WebGPU variant.
author: Spiri0
Spiri0

body: I don't think we have implement the entire uniform mechanism for WebGPU compute, so it could be a uniform bug in specific to `WebGPURenderer.compute()`, I'll be checking that.
author: sunag

body:
Seems like I can look forward to r158 then. There don't seem to be many people who are concerned with webgpu and the node system yet. But it's pretty elegant and saves code. It makes things easier. I tell myself that if interest in webgpu increases quickly, all of these points will already anticipate a lot.
I hope my codePen example is helpful and I'm curious to read what your analysis reveals. Mine surprised me and reassured me at the same time.
I can imagine my IFFT system as a module for three.js. This would then be easy to use for all possible frequency images so that users do not have to deal with the demanding IFFT mechanics themselves. I do this because I want a real ocean because for land I use real maps. But I could also easily create much more natural-looking artificial landscapes with my IFFT system than is possible with the usual noise functions.
author: Spiri0

body: I think the concerned is because the system of nodes and `TSL` is something very new for many, it is not documented and `WebGPURenderer` and `TSL` is a WIP, comparisons usually between `WebGLRenderer` and `WebGPURenderer` are about the strengths of `WebGLRenderer` that have been polished over many years, we are closing several issues per month and I believe it is a matter of time before everything is resolved and we have the best of both worlds, and certainly projects like yours speed up development, makes us think about different needs and solutions and it does all the deep testing work which there was no time to do it.
author: sunag

body:
I think that might have been the last obstacle in my implementation. The pingpong routine in which I need the uniform for the current iteration step is the last step in the IFFT calculation. The progress in r156 and r157 was already clearly noticeable for me. I think this covers most of the cases that users will ever have to deal with when generating textures with compute shaders. 

Not that I'm conjuring up the devil with my confidence, who will then confront me again with the next bug. But if that's the case, so be it.
author: Spiri0

body:
In the forum I see that other people are also interested in the node system and that makes me happy. Since there is still some time until r158, I thought I would take a look at what could possibly cause problems. Instead of THREE.HalfFloatType I tested THREE.FloatType and rgba32float in the shader. rgba16float is enough for me but I just wanted to see if rgba32float also works. That doesn't work and warnings appear in the console that remind me of #26576. But that's more of a luxury problem because with rgba16float I can do a lot of things and everything that I can currently imagine. 
I'm really looking forward to r158.

author: mrdoob
mrdoob

body:
> Exposing Attributes in WebGLRenderer to gain access to reference to GL buffers.

Instead of exposing `attributes`, you could just use the existing [GLBufferAttribute](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#api/en/core/GLBufferAttribute) class?

I'm not a fan of exposing the internals of the renderer so I hope a different solution like `GLBufferAttribute` could do the trick.
author: Mugen87

body:
> > Exposing Attributes in WebGLRenderer to gain access to reference to GL buffers.
> 
> Instead of exposing `attributes`, you could just use the existing [GLBufferAttribute](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#api/en/core/GLBufferAttribute) class?

Didn't know about GLBufferAttributes. Looking at the doc, looks like I have to create the gl buffers myself using the GL context then pass it to the object. This complicates things as I'm modifying threejs existing primitives & instance meshes with compute shaders. All the attribute buffers are created by threejs, using GLBufferAttribute means I'll have to create custom version of those things. In the event the context is lost, all those geometries will need to be recreated from scratch again if I had to manage the buffers whereas it is now, I'm using modified version of 3js in production, it handles that bit itself, I just need to recreate the compute shader & data texture.

I understand not exposing the internals of the renderer, but its already being done with 'properties' in the same function I'd like to expose attributes in. I currently use properties to get the GL reference to framebuffers to do custom things like using webgl's blitFrameBuffer API. If framebuffers are exposed, why not regular buffers.
author: sketchpunk

body:
> If framebuffers are exposed, why not regular buffers.

That is a fair point but `properties` was exposed at a time where we wasn't aware about the related consequences. Or in other words, doing things wrong in the past does not justify doing things wrong now.

I was hoping having a dedicated API at some point like outlined here: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/14503#issuecomment-950887344

In the meanwhile, I would prefer to use the options we already have.
author: Mugen87

body:
> In the meanwhile, I would prefer to use the options we already have.

Thats the thing, the options available don't really help. In the examples I provided, GLBufferAttribute wouldn't be a solution, instead it would complicate matters by needing to create custom version of primitives & InstancedMesh. I've done things like that in the past, a few 3js versions out things break that I'd have to fix up my custom versions of threejs objects.

To me, this is the path of least resistance to utilize compute shaders on existing threejs features.
author: sketchpunk

body:
I think when we see the renderer losing control of the backend calls, it is because something could be improved. I would not like to see the WebGPU API exposed in the WebGPU Examples... On the other hand, the example is well done and can temporarily help until a Three.js API is oriented towards this. I don't know how much impact this would have, @Mugen87 would know how to respond better.

About `WebGPURenderer`, I think `Transformfeedback` should be oriented to be used in `WebGLBackend.compute()`, because `WebGPURenderer.compute()` is aimed at this kind of solution.
author: sunag

body:
Curious, whats the final decision on this? Accepted or not?

If it does, I'll like to attempt two more examples. One doing Dual Quaternion Skinning & maybe animation with compute shader while threejs renders a character as a static mesh without using the built in skinning features.

The other example using a procedurally generated spring mesh, how to properly compress & expand it. Doing regular scaling stretches the mesh which doesn't look right. 

Maybe these 4 test cases can be useful for further developing an abstraction layer down the line to properly run transformfeedback in threejs.
author: sketchpunk

body:
I'm not at liberty to share details, but I've used transform feedback in my prior work which is somewhat publicized in https://twitter.com/onirenaud/status/1592183793548677125. I'd be happy to help champion or clear remaining blockers, but I don't recall this feature being particularly involved.

Edit: I see that almost no work needs to be done if we're to rely on internal `attributes`? I'm not super happy about requiring WebGL code anywhere in user-land though.

> About `WebGPURenderer`, I think `Transformfeedback` should be oriented to be used in `WebGLBackend.compute()`, because `WebGPURenderer.compute()` is aimed at this kind of solution.

I think so too since they provide similar benefits with GPU-side work and how precious texture memory is, although we don't get fancy storage memory or real compute access with shared memory in WebGL. I've even modeled compute in my toy library after it since I could offer an identical API and examples https://github.com/CodyJasonBennett/four#compute. I'm working on a few GPU-driven examples which I wanted to port to WebGL 2 to prove the similarity, but in three.js I think this and uniform buffers (I'm not sure if we cleared every issue?) could use some work to at least serve as a stepping stone to WebGPU.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
> Edit: I see that almost no work needs to be done if we're to rely on internal `attributes`? I'm not super happy about requiring WebGL code anywhere in user-land though.

Yes, it's a quick & dirty solution but it's better than nothing at all. I know there are issues doing this, like Vao bindings aren't being cleaned up correctly by the 3js renderer, so I have to clear it before I do any sort of compute or meshes will be harmed in the process. In my opinion, this is for someone who needs compute now & has some know-how of webgl itself. In our case, we really need compute as it was the only solution to a problem, so now we run a modified version of the library in production just so we can get access to attributes, this itself isn't ideal but we don't have much of a choice.

Since its a tiny change & it mirrors "properties" which I also use in production to access framebuffer references to use with webgl's blitFramebuffer functionality since 3js doesn't have support for it.  If there is no support for transformfeedback then the only thing people need is access to buffer references to be able to read & write into them which exposing attributes will do.

body:
The code below cannot return the same vector, see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26493#discussion_r1313456485, https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26493#discussion_r1315850641
```js
vec = vec.xy.assign( 1 );
```

--

The code below is good!
```js
variable.xy = vec2( 1, 2 );
```

Is it possible to you simplify this PR to support just this?
author: sunag

body: Would this be compatible with `stack.if`?
author: sunag
sunag

body:
> The code below cannot return the same vector

Yeah, I understand now that it looks quite confusing... Maybe we could say that the "main" approach is to do `vec.abc = something` and the other one is also supported, but don't use it in examples? Like in the same way we have two approach for nodes, node elements (preferred) and "raw" node classes (not really preferred, but still supported).

> Would this be compatible with stack.if?

I think the problem here is in `VarNode.assign()`... It currently works in "compile-time" (JS code), maybe we should change its behavior to work in "run-time" (shader code) so that it would be more in line with other methods.
author: LeviPesin

body: I don't think we should support this syntax `vec = vec.xy.assign( 1 )`, if we could shorten the code it would be better.
author: sunag

body: I don't really see how to support the "bonus" syntax without supporting this one...
author: LeviPesin

body:
I'm afraid with the latest changes to VarNode.assign() the "bonus" syntax can no longer work... The `var = var.z.assign( var.w )` still works, though.
@sunag Is there any way we could enable the bonus syntax to work again? I don't really see such...
author: LeviPesin

body: There is an approach that could work, although we would have to find a good way to do it. This would be having a global stack reference, like `currentStack`, if we make this work we can have nodes like `assign/if/else/loop` without needing to use `stack.assign` or `stack.if` for example, so we can do everything without having to use `stack` property. I've had this in mind for some time but haven't tested it yet.

body:
I had to rebase to add support for `InterleavedBufferAttribute` to the existing code...

Would you have an example to reproduce the bug? 

One of the reasons I delayed the merge of the previous PR was the `geometry.id`, other thing is that `NodeMaterial` can also add anonymous attributes. I think it's better to improve the caching system instead of bringing this logic to the `draw()`.
author: sunag

body:
The addition of the geometry.id in the previous PR solves the problem in the occlusion example where both objects use the same material and the second object gets the VAO from the previous object unless they use different pipeline objects.

I spotted the problem of additional non geometry attributes so I cached VAO by RenderObject rather than using geometry.id in this PR. I still think the draw call is the correct place because it should allow pipelines to be used for multiple objects.  Attributes are set up per draw call in the WebGPU equivalent code.
author: aardgoose

body:
The problem is that this is not passing through the cache logic, I agree with you about `geometry.id` and I explained in the comment above, other reasons, I think replacing `renderObject.geometry.id` with `renderObject.id` in `getCacheKey()` could give the same result provisionally and saves this check in `draw()`, I would merge this if it works until we improve the cache.

If we follow that each `RenderObject` will have a `VAO` independent, we will never have shared `VAO`, as we have a `RenderObject` for each `Mesh`, regardless of whether it has the same `Geometry` and `Material`.
author: sunag

body:
I have finally worked what problem this addresses. The webgpu_materials_video example required a new gl.linkProgram() for each cube rendered, although only two shader objects were created  (vertex and fragment) which results in a noticeable delay when starting the video. 

I agree ideally vaos would be cached by something that captured the combination of geometry and material generated attributes.

body:
Actually you should never manually specify the GLSL version when using `RawShaderMaterial`. You are supposed to do this:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/ce0b3d0415c4bc87a8b4ad01c5d21cc26c6ec5eb/examples/webgl2_volume_cloud.html#L255-L256
author: Mugen87

body:
This is a serious problem for me. I just updated one of my many project that extensively uses RawMaterialShader & everything is completely broken because of this #define issue. The whole reason why I used RawShaderMaterial over ShaderMaterial was the promise or assumption in its name, its 100% my glsl code without the library screwing with it. The word "RAW" means as is, no changes, the original. Once you start inserting things in it, its no longer raw code, its modified code.

I recommend this issue be opened & fixed, I really shouldn't have to fix 16 projects ( Personal & Professional ) because the library starts to modify my raw glsl code.
author: sketchpunk

body: I recognize that this could have been incidentally enabled prior with the lack of version headers needed for GLSL ES 1 (the default `glslVersion`), but people reach for `RawShaderMaterial` for portability with other systems and I'd suggest such as a legitimate use-case to leave their shaders unmodified. This might include shader tools/libraries like https://github.com/patriciogonzalezvivo/lygia or native IDE integrations for IntelliSense where a version is required to distinguish language features.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: @mrdoob @Mugen87 Maybe we can make it so that `glslVersion` is auto set when `#version` is specified?
author: LeviPesin

body:
> I recognize that this could have been incidentally enabled prior with the lack of version headers needed for GLSL ES 1 (the default glslVersion), 

This change was done on purpose after a series of issues and PRs. Please read #19959 with the related issues and PRs to understand the status quo.

> The whole reason why I used RawShaderMaterial over ShaderMaterial was the promise or assumption in its name, its 100% my glsl code without the library screwing with it.

This assumptions isn't right since the engine does post-process the GLSL code e.g. to integrate defines, extensions, resolve `#include <>` statements or to integrate the version string.
author: Mugen87

body:
> This change was done on purpose after a series of issues and PRs. Please read #19959 with the related issues and PRs to understand the status quo.
> 
From what I understand, the PRs are to fix issues caused by pre-appending defines, which shouldn't have been done in the first place. I do not understand what problems defines for shader_type and shader_name solves, it sure doesn't help run GLSL better or faster. If this is for some sort of tagging maybe the better solution is to append it at the end where it'll cause no issues.

> This assumptions isn't right since the engine does post-process the GLSL code e.g. to integrate defines, resolve `#include <>` statements or to integrate the version string.

Again, the defines added to the glsl doesn't seem useful for anything in relation to raw shaders. Secondly, I'm ok with #includes if I choose to have them in there but if I don't use includes then I expect the GLSL to be executed as is. Plus the version string isn't integrated if its null which is the default value. You can see its not there in the error messages I get before the defines.

If people need to write shaders with some hand holding, that's what ShaderMaterial is for. I've always seen RawShaderMaterial like rust's **"UNSAFE"** feature. There are points when a developer needs the system to back off for a bit & let the dev run the code that they need.

My biggest issue with 3JS as a whole is that there isn't enough thought about letting people access to bits that makes it easier to use webgl features directly. Like blitz to transfer framebuffer content isn't something the library supports, so I need to circumvent the library to use it. Transformfeedback, I need to run a modified version of the library at meta to use compute shaders because buffer references aren't allowed & after 6 years of the feature being available in Webgl2, it's still not usable in 3JS & I honestly cant sit around waiting for it to be available. Along with other smaller bits in the last 5 years of using the library. So adding these defines into raw shaders is just another example of the system getting in the way.

![F8QLQxGXQAYoJ9W](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/2301158/90c91208-895a-4928-be84-7566eddbfbb8)
author: sketchpunk

body:
Would it be helpful for you to have a flag in `RawShaderMaterial` that when set to `true`, the following code block is skipped:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/da475225a07d86874c605907ea46975f213d124c/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLProgram.js#L460-L490 

Includes could still be resolved but apart from than nothing would be changed in the GLSL source.

body: If that flag default state was true, sure. Leaving the original behavior intact while telling the dev that if they need these defines & auto inserting the version, they need to opt-in.

I'd also be fine with changing KTX2Exporter to an API like...

```js
exporter.parse( renderTarget, { renderer } );
```

... if that works better for EXRExporter too.
author: donmccurdy
sciecode

body:
I'm fine with it either way! If there started to be *more* required arguments for certain cases, like `parse( x, y, z, w, options )` then I would certainly roll them into the options object if they're only conditionally required. Also for async exporters you avoid this stuff:

```js
parse( x, y, ..., onLoad, onError, options );
```

Maybe best to just match what KTX2Exporter does for now yes, and we can make changes if the pattern isn't working well for other exporters.

body:
>  the try-catch cannot catch err raised from .decodeAudioData() which returns a promise.

I 'm not sure this is right. The try/catch block was added on purpose. The following code example at MDN (section Promise-based syntax) also uses try/catch around `decodeAudioData()`.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/BaseAudioContext/decodeAudioData

Did you actually test the PR with a malformed audio file? If so, it would be great to share a live example that demonstrates how the current implementation does not enter the catch block.

/cc @luisfonsivevo
author: Mugen87

body:
Mdn showed two usages of that api, one is [promise-based](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/BaseAudioContext/decodeAudioData#promise-based_syntax), err is handled by try-catch(in async ctx) or then-catch-apis(not in async ctx); another is [callback-based](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/BaseAudioContext/decodeAudioData#callback_syntax), err is handled by ..callbacks. This is thus the try-catch is redundant as the current(r156) [FileLoader's onLoad callback](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/ce0b3d0415c4bc87a8b4ad01c5d21cc26c6ec5eb/src/loaders/AudioLoader.js#L22) is not in async ctx

> Did you actually test the PR with a malformed audio file?

of course..

author: ycw
Mugen87

body:
All right, uptight, out of sight! (looks good)
Agree about the try/catch block still being useful though since there are other ways for the file loader callback to fail. As this PR stands, we're running the (small) risk of the audio loader hanging forever like it used to.
author: luisfonsivevo

body:
> other ways for the file loader callback to fail

how?

> ... audio loader hanging forever

try-catch wont protect from it neither..
author: ycw

body:
Admittedly it's very unlikely to fail compared to `decodeAudioData`, but for example:
- `window.AudioContext` could be undefined due to the browser not supporting WebAudio
- `buffer.slice()` fails for some reason as @Mugen87 mentioned

You can see it hanging here: https://jsfiddle.net/t1w08xeu/3/
author: luisfonsivevo

body:
> window.AudioContext could be undefined due to the browser not supporting WebAudio

[This line clearly showed that the AudioContext is assumed to be well supported](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/309e5f6f64c7af9087e0fb6f7cbf83a9fd2a4fef/src/audio/AudioContext.js#L9).

If this is not the case, 'audio/AudioContext.js' should do feature detection before constructing an AudioContext, then store `_context = null` if missing, s.t. subs.`audioLoader.load()` won't get TypeError again and again; Also AudioLoader `.load()` should check if audiocontext is missing (`if (AudioContext.getContext() === null) ...`) **BEFORE** `fileloader.load(..)` to prevent wasteful network activities, i.e. no need of try-catch in fileloader's onLoad callback at all.

> buffer.slice() fails for some reason as @Mugen87 mentioned

would you mind name the exact exception(s) which is possible raised by `arraybuffer.slice()`?
author: ycw

body:
> if so, why its not thrown from AudioContext(the singleton)?

It is thrown. For some reason you can't see the error in the jsfiddle console but if you open DevTools it's there:
![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/19977842/cfdd3424-2608-4cb1-aff1-cf3dafc14c3a)
> could you mind point out the exact exception(s) will be thrown?

I had to enter a massive number to get a screenshot, but someone who's low on RAM could encounter an out of memory error:
![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/19977842/347b3c5a-be03-417a-abd2-979a9917ab75)
author: luisfonsivevo

body: Thanks for your time! this PR is closed; reason: AudioLoader is intended to handle 1) TypeError due to AudioContext is not supported, and 2) RangeError due to out-of-memory when slicing gigantic arraybuffer; So, even if the try-catch is unable to catch async err raised from `.decodeAudioData()`, its still needed to catch the sync errs caused by 1) and 2).
author: ycw
luisfonsivevo

body:
yup, ~~but make sure that onerror is called on next frame..~~

I prefer [fixing this issue](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26816#issuecomment-1729984414) first:

current(r156) impl issue:

```
// AudioLoader.js

load(url, onLoad, onProgress, onError) {
  load audio as ab -> onload: decode abCopy using audiocontext, or throw if audiocontext not supported.
  # if audiocontext not supported, then audioloader should not fetch the audio at the beginning
  #  otherwise, network activitvies as well as ab slicing are wasteful, and repeats for each `audioloader.load(..)`
```

body:
Just a couple thoughts while passing through -

- It looks like this is implementing SDF to geometry functionality with "Surface Nets" which is basically a Marching Cubes alternative. It would be nice to align the API with the current [MarchingCubes](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/objects/MarchingCubes.js) object implementation since in theory surface nets should be a drop in replacement if I'm understanding right. Though the current MarchingCubes class is structured a bit oddly extending from `Mesh` so maybe this can be figured out later.
- It looks like a lot of the code is copy-pasted from [this library file here](https://github.com/mikolalysenko/isosurface/blob/master/lib/surfacenets.js) in another repo. It might make most sense to drop that file (modified to support exports) in the `jsm/libs` directory with the other library files along with a date of copy / version number so it's easier to keep track of.

Overall surface nets look cool, though - I hadn't heard of them before!

body: @santi-grau While improving the example, I have noticed `SDFGeometryGenerator` makes no use of `dispose()` methods which means the component creates memory leaks. I have refactored the code a bit to fix this issue. You can easily see the effect when inspecting the `renderer.info` debug object after generating some geometries.
author: Mugen87

body:
I've realized the rendering performance of `computeSDF()` could be noticeably improved by using instanced rendering because the geometry (the plane) is the same for each draw call and only the `tileNum` uniform is different. If `tileNum` and the position of the plane would be instanced attributes, `computeSDF()` would only require a single draw call, no matter how many tiles are configured.

Would you be willing to give this implementation a try?
author: Mugen87

body:
> @santi-grau While improving the example, I have noticed `SDFGeometryGenerator` makes no use of `dispose()` methods which means the component creates memory leaks. I have refactored the code a bit to fix this issue. You can easily see the effect when inspecting the `renderer.info` debug object after generating some geometries.

Didn't know we had to manually dispose. Thanks for adding!
author: santi-grau

body:
> I've realized the rendering performance of `computeSDF()` could be noticeably improved by using instanced rendering because the geometry (the plane) is the same for each draw call and only the `tileNum` uniform is different. If `tileNum` and the position of the plane would be instanced attributes, `computeSDF()` would only require a single draw call, no matter how many tiles are configured.
> 
> Would you be willing to give this implementation a try?

The reason why I'm using 'tile rendering' is to prevent crashes in case the SDF function gets too complex ( e.g. fbm ). This way, no matter what, we are just computing a specific tile size. If you still think that instance rendering will still be safe and more efficient I can try to implement it.
author: santi-grau

body:
> The reason why I'm using 'tile rendering' is to prevent crashes in case the SDF function gets too complex

I see. In this case, I think it's okay to start with the current implemented approach. If users report performance issues, we can revisit this topic (and maybe think about different rendering modes).

body: In that case it was specifically discussed about `src/` directory, because the consequences of even small risks of regressions there outweighs the benefits of small code refactors. In this case this PR is cleaning up an addon and make the code very much more clean and understandable compared to how it was before. I splitted the refactoring in individual commits to make reviewing the changes easier.

author: mrdoob

body:
> So yeah... scene.background should be tonemapped too.

This would make my life a lot easier, and this solves one of the problems I'm having when porting transmission materials node-based. To work correctly, the process must work in `linear color space` while rendering objects and only at the output apply `tone mapping` and `output color space`.
author: sunag

body:
I agree this is the more correct way to do things! But there will unfortunately be users with problems because they need the background to match something in HTML/CSS. I wish we had some way to apply the inverse of the tone mapping to a color, so users can at least compute a fog or background color that will give them the final background color they want.

Many colors in the sRGB gamut cannot be reached by ACES tone mapping outputs though, so this would also limit your choice of background color. Perhaps AgX will be more flexible there, but I'm not sure.

Or perhaps THREE.Fog could take an alpha value?
author: donmccurdy

body: I like the idea of rendering with `alpha` and applying it in `Fog` too, I think it could solve this issue with HTML/CSS? since the transparent background can provide much more possibilities than a single color. This principle could also follow this reasoning https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26857#issuecomment-1737487823

body:
What are the open points in this PR? Do we need add an `alpha` property to the fog classes?

As mentioned earlier, the status quo is especially confusing when devs use post processing with a scene using fog. As soon as they integrate `OutputPass` in their pass chain, the scene looks suddenly different compared to the _inline_ tone mapping and color space conversion. Most devs initially think FX is buggy although it actually renders the scene correctly (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/26954#issuecomment-1759574593).

body: Another alternative could be using `renderer.coordinateSystem`.
author: sunag

body:
How would you describe what `camera.coordinateSystem` represents?

```js
renderer.backend:  WebGPUBackend
renderer.coordinateSystem:  2001
camera.coordinateSystem:  2000
```
author: WestLangley

body:
This happens because the default value of `camera.coordinateSystem` is `WebGLCoordinateSystem`, after the first rendering this value is updated for the chosen backend.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/8581f7937047d72f91927961f2517e4ee826e40b/examples/jsm/renderers/common/Renderer.js#L199-L207
author: sunag

body:
I'm just trying to determine if the design may be too fragile... thanks for bearing with me. :-)

Are there any situations in which the renderer must be instantiated prior to the camera -- or the camera prior to the renderer?
author: WestLangley

body:
It would not have priority in terms of instantiation, both can be declared before or after, `renderer.coordinateSystem` will always be the predominant value while `camera.coordinateSystem` must adapt to the renderer's backend. `camera.coordinateSystem` should not be recommended as a strong value for backend comparison unlike `renderer.coordinateSystem`.

It will always be a pleasure for me to refine any stage with you. :-)

Looks like Unity uses them green at the top and Unreal green at the bottom. I'm neutral on the default. But do feel we should include the option. author: NikLever

body: > For Unity it looks like this is only supported via "Amplify Color" for handling LUTs and not baked into the engine, right? The fact that green is swapped vertically also strikes me as odd. Though it looks like Unreal also [supports using lut strips](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FCzoIvnpCso). The Unreal LUT documentation and available neutral LUT strip is [here](https://docs.unrealengine.com/4.27/en-US/RenderingAndGraphics/PostProcessEffects/UsingLUTs/). Unity URP has a post-processing filter that you can add to a Volume, Color Look-up. It is described in the e-book I wrote for Unity. https://resources.unity.com/ebooks/the-universal-render-pipeline-cookbook-unity-2022-lts-edition?ungated=true https://blog.unity.com/engine-platform/12-recipes-for-popular-visual-effects-using-universal-render-pipeline Green bottom
author: NikLever

body:
> I just double checked and a neutral affect is created with the NeutralLUT having green at the top

I believe that Unity expects green increasing bottom to top - I was just trying to reconcile it with the video linked. But I see now that that video is about writing a custom shaders.

> should I change the application to use the second parameter and not the default? Maybe add a comment about unreal and unity.

I think given that unreal and the three.js manual uses increasing green from top to bottom and an unofficial Unity uses the reverse means that we should default to non flipped and we can flip the images.

> This is my first contribution to the library and I'm very impressed with how rigorous the approval process is :-)

Heh file formats formats and standards like this tend to be a sticking point for me. Thanks for bearing with me. I've been bit by having to support weird non-standard features or ill-defined specifications in the past so I like to try to understand and get it right up front where possible. I think a big project has a responsibility to not contribute to the proliferation of new or non standard formats that users may have the expectation of other engines supporting in the future. To be honest I'm a bit annoyed that both Unity and Unreal use this image format instead of the CUBE or 3DL formats that the rest of the color grading tools seem to use and support... And even worse that they're not consistent.

author: WestLangley

body:
The [spec requires UA to do this](https://w3c.github.io/mathml-core/#fractions-mfrac):

> ... To avoid visual confusion between the fraction bar and another adjacent items (e.g. minus sign or another fraction's bar), a default 1-pixel space is added around the element. The [user agent stylesheet](https://w3c.github.io/mathml-core/#dfn-user-agent-stylesheet) must contain the following rules: ...

but safari misbehaved, so a patch is needed

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26896/commits/8a78521e126229845264b8f629813463a27bb5e2: `mspace` is semantically correct, and supported since safari7, but this option will cause visually diff btwn safari and non-safari (space is much wider in non-safari)

Lets try another option

[sha]: adds a dot operator between `mfrac`s. The dot in safari should behave like the minus in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26896#issuecomment-1745573220 which has proper space added around; U+22C5 is semantically correct, but the equations now looks more 'verbose' (a dot is added between the const and derivatives)

body:
Resizing while presenting is currently blocked, but otherwise window events wouldn't come up for immersive sessions and only inline/emulator.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/3eec0e76b235c7c4648feee76e9841ec1c9a6899/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L424-L431

I would expect `WebGLRenderer.getDrawingBufferSize` to align with the current drawing surface, and `WebGLRenderer.getSize` by extension. Although I wouldn't be opposed to XR-specific methods, it breaks that expectation.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
> Resizing while presenting is currently blocked, but otherwise window events wouldn't come up for immersive sessions and only inline/emulator.

I didn't realize that - that behavior seems a bit odd to me. Is there an issue on why that was needed? What happens if the web browser is resized during an XR session (ie in desktop VR)? This must mean the canvas doesn't have the correct scale on exiting VR? Maybe I'm misunderstanding something.

> I would expect WebGLRenderer.getDrawingBufferSize to align with the current drawing surface, and WebGLRenderer.getSize by extension

This isn't how it works currently, though, I don't feel? When the draw target is set to a WebGLRenderTarget, for example, `getSize` and `setSize` still return and set the canvas size rather than the render target. 

If everyone else thinks this approach is best, though, then we should merge it!
author: gkjohnson

body:
> I didn't realize that - that behavior seems a bit odd to me. Is there an issue on why that was needed? What happens if the web browser is resized during an XR session (ie in desktop VR)? This must mean the canvas doesn't have the correct scale on exiting VR? Maybe I'm misunderstanding something.

I'm tracing that back to d981806124472c6c50d8306b56f78ccbbbadf5ea of [r86](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/releases/tag/r86) which was before WebXR -- WebVR. Looks to be related to #13225.

> This isn't how it works currently, though, I don't feel? When the draw target is set to a WebGLRenderTarget, for example, getSize and setSize still return and set the canvas size rather than the render target.

It is only when targeting the underlying drawing surface or the `null` target (related: #26160). In apps, this is measured for presentation purposes since the final output must be compatible (e.g. post-processing, upscaling). Viewport state is not reliable in this manner since it can and will change per draw call. The same is true for render targets which normally wouldn't correspond to the drawing surface whatsoever, but with layers the internal render target is a necessary implementation detail.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
> This must mean the canvas doesn't have the correct scale on exiting VR?

I'm having trouble setting up my oculus at the moment to test due to some account issues - but I'm still curious about what happens when you resize the page and then exit VR.

> In apps, this is measured for presentation purposes since the final output must be compatible (e.g. post-processing, upscaling).

I understand why this is necessary. I'm just suggesting that it could be the apps responsibility to measure or pick what it's target drawing surface is and adjust accordingly - which could be the canvas, a render target, or XR layer. If you actually can't adjust the canvas during an XR session then this implementation makes sense.

Either way this can be tested later or reevaluated if it causes an issue. Let's merge it.
author: gkjohnson

author: gkjohnson

body: Making a PR that just adds the `@__PURE__` annotations sounds like a good start. Most of the diff should be gone if we have these changes in which makes it easier to review the other code.
author: Mugen87

body:
This now tree-shakes all of #26910 which excludes WebGPU and Nodes for the moment (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26910#discussion_r1349555522).

I've used that PR to incrementally test changes here with `npx agadoo@latest examples/jsm/Addons.js`.

Changes include adding `@__PURE__` annotations around top-level expressions, wrapping tricky code in a pure IIFE (such as libs), and moving top-level mutations to pure `Object.assign`. Since some usage of the `static` keyword already existed in WebXR for instance, that can be broken up into another PR to filter out a lot of nonsense diffing from whitespace and whatnot.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
> Since some usage of the static keyword already existed in WebXR

I think it's because the usage of static functions is fine but not static fields?
author: LeviPesin

body: Indeed. That tidies things up a little. Static methods are as back as ES6, but static fields are ES 2022.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
There seems to be three recurring patterns used in this PR:

1. Using the `/* @__PURE__ */` annotation when creating instances of `THREE` objects. This is a know pattern from the core.
2. Using `Object.assign()` (with `/* @__PURE__ */`) to assign fields the classes.
3. Wrap certain code in IIFEs like when using `UniformsUtils.merge()` in `MeshGouraudMaterial`.

I'm familiar with the first one since we are already using this approach in the core. 

Do you mind explain 2 and 3? It's important that all collaborators (including myself^^) fully understand these patterns to ensure maintenance. Especially the IIFE thing is something that could easily be overlooked in new code that is going to be added to the library.
author: Mugen87
CodyJasonBennett

body:
> I ran into a similar issue with Nodes, but that was more complicated since a nodes registry had to be populated despite no actual reference (thus everything is effectful).

Related (in some way) issue: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25526
author: LeviPesin

body:
Looks like the latter case in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26912#issuecomment-1752998540 is true. This adds a lot of whitespace nonsense, but otherwise the files are unmodified. Still, this would be good to split up since the rest of this PR very cleanly adds annotations.

Edit: I've since removed the whitespace changes, but they will need to be formatted with a linter. Actual code changes should just be the scope for the IIFE.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: I've also recapped everything found in review in the PR description for future reference with some notes on future quality control via linting. For this PR and future reference, I've written a CLI tool to E2E test with bundlers and diagnostics which I describe further there.

body: TBH, I'm not really a fan of the `Fat*` syntax. I would call the class `InstancedSprite` (and `InstancedSpriteGeometry` and `InstancedSpriteMaterial`).
author: Mugen87

body: Sidenote: At some point we should maybe rename examples like `webgl_lines_fat` to `webgl_lines_wide` or something similar.
author: Mugen87

body:
> I would call the class `InstancedSprite`

But these are not sprites. They are points, sized in pixels. When re-implemented in TSL, they would replace the single-pixel points rendered by `WebGPURenderer`.

Instanced sprites have a map, are sized in world units, can be size-attenuated, and can be rotated.

I am inclined to think we are talking about two different things.

> I'm not really a fan of the Fat* syntax.

That's fine. Different nomenclature can be used.
author: WestLangley

body: We can also call them `InstancedPoints`. I think we originally did want to use the "points" term because they are not based on the points primitive.
author: Mugen87

body:
Regarding https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25697#issuecomment-1482202559:

With this implementation, is the point size now consistent when changing the renderer's pixel ratio?
author: Mugen87

body:
>With this implementation, is the point size now consistent when changing the renderer's pixel ratio?

The user should not have to worry about the device pixels.

`pointWidth` here is in CSS Pixels, and the point size is invariant to `window.devicePixelRatio`.
author: WestLangley

body:
> pointWidth here is in CSS Pixels, and the point size is invariant to window.devicePixelRatio.

That is good since it solves one issue with previous point primitive rendering.
author: Mugen87

body:
`WebGPURenderer` is now supported. Many thanks to @aardgoose for #26704.

The "Fat Points" nomenclature can be changed.
author: WestLangley

body: Please remove the `Fat*` syntax though. I vote for `InstancedPoints*`.

body:
I've been testing with `npx agadoo@latest` against addons from #26910, only trying individual tools to ensure correct build output.

Perhaps this isn't good enough. Prior, I setup https://github.com/CodyJasonBennett/treeshaking to test misc. behaviors.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
I would like to continue to copy libs into the repository. Not all developers use `npm` to host the code. If we introduce real (non-dev) dependencies, we force all users towards `npm`.

Besides, certain libs perform too much logging or inconsistent error handling that contradicts `three.js` policies. In this case, it's more clean to modify the library to provide users a more consistent experience.  Good examples for this are `UTIF` or `UPNG` which are embedded in loaders.

> instead of needing to copy, maintain, format and fix them in the Three.js' repository

Looking back at 8 years of maintenance, upgrading third party libs was never an issue. The copy is fast, formatting happens automatically if wanted, and the number of third party libs is small. Besides, we never blindly upgrade dependencies and there is no pressure to upgrade to each patch or minor release.
author: Mugen87

body:
> Good examples for this are UTIF or UPNG which are embedded in loaders.

We can continue packaging them, but why we couldn't use, for example, external `fflate` or `mmd-parser`?
author: LeviPesin

body:
@CodyJasonBennett hmmm not sure about the inner workings of that tool, it is better to test with the bundler directly in my opinion.

I have setup a repo similar to yours if it's useful, with esbuild + rollup + webpack.
https://github.com/marcofugaro/treeshake-test

Make sure to import from the `node_modules` since bundlers treat app code differently from vendor code regarding tree-shaking. I often use `yarn link`.
author: marcofugaro

body: @LeviPesin We can solve the tree-shaking issues without changing the policy how external libraries are managed.
author: Mugen87

body: I'll leave it to @takahirox as for whether this is preferable for code-style or other reasons, but for tree-shaking this PR proves unneeded and comically difficult to review. Thanks @marcofugaro for the assistance and direction.

body: @takahirox should we update the mmd examples to user the `mmd-parser` npm and unpkg?
author: mrdoob

body:
Regarding ES6, `mmd-parser` was written very long time ago. It's nice to rewrite in ES6. Probably doing it at the original repo https://github.com/takahirox/mmd-parser, and then copy or import is good. PR is very welcome.

Regarding npm or unpkg, I think it should be aligned with our external libs policy. If other libs are imported via npm/unpkg, `mmd-parser` should be, too. If they are not, `mmd-parser` should not be.

body:
Side note: Stencil for the default drawing buffer is enabled by default (since the `stencil` WebGL context attribute is `true` by default). A user reported now a performance degradation when we sync the stencil buffer of the transmission render target with the `stencil` context attribute, see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/27846#issuecomment-1985415152.

Let's clarify in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27799#issuecomment-1985538145 first if we can set `stencil` to `false` by default.

author: Mugen87

body:
Unfortunately, the PR changes multi-touch behavior. If you use two fingers for dragging, the existing version supports dragging until all fingers are lifted. In the new version, dragging is stopped after a single finger is lifted. That's because `status` does not count pointers anymore.

I think it would be better to find out why `status` isn't correctly decreased in some cases.  Can you please check if using `pointercancel` as an additional event for executing `_pointerup()` solves the issue? https://jsfiddle.net/aq8nhgv3/1/show

We had a similar issue in `OrbitControls` and using `pointercancel` was the solution.
author: Mugen87

body:
I see the reason to use a counter now, thanks!

Sadly i could not reproduce the bug for now but had a browser update in the meantime. Still i would suspect pointercancel should set the state to zero to be sure, not decrement the counter? From the MDN i understand this event fires for several reasons like switching the device orientation, accidential input etc. but yeah it would prevent this bug for sure (in a separate function though, not via additional _pointerup).

Thanks for taking the time looking into this as this descent-style camera is loads of fun and counting the number of fingers allows for additions of roll, etc.!
author: krei-se

body:
> Still i would suspect pointercancel should set the state to zero to be sure, not decrement the counter? 

Um, I think you are right there. Previously, I have assumed `pointercancel` is fired per active pointer but it seems more of a global event. Yes, in this case, setting to `0` is more correct. I'll file a PR.
author: Mugen87

body:
I made a simple `ShaderToyDecoder` and  `ShaderToyNode` to show some different use cases.

body: Note to reviewers — EXT_materials_bump is a proposal, until it is implemented by at least two vendors. If we feel `.bumpMap` would be a valuable addition to glTF for three.js users, we can merge this PR and be one of those vendors. If we have concerns about supporting `.bumpMap` in glTF, now would be a good time to raise them. In the linked pull request, @bhouston gives use cases for the extension.
author: donmccurdy

body: @donmccurdy I can confirm that Threekit will support this extension.
author: bhouston

body: Lets hope Khronos approves this one soon 🤞
author: mrdoob

body:
Usually EXT_ extensions are not subject to any Khronos approval – they're added to the repository and considered out of the proposal stage if >=2 vendors have added support. EXT_ extensions are less 'official', which can be a good thing if there are important limitations associated with the extension, or a concept just needs to be proved.


Perhaps Khronos may express support or concerns, but many PRs just sit without responses, and I do not expect an official response here. If we feel this will benefit three.js users then that is enough, and we can ship it I think. Though if it's only three.js and ThreeKit for now, perhaps the extension should be `THREE_*` rather than `EXT_*` unless/until other vendors add support.
author: donmccurdy

body:
@donmccurdy here is the list of other EXT_ extensions that ThreeJS supports:

```js
	EXT_TEXTURE_WEBP: 'EXT_texture_webp',
	EXT_TEXTURE_AVIF: 'EXT_texture_avif',
	EXT_MESHOPT_COMPRESSION: 'EXT_meshopt_compression',
	EXT_MESH_GPU_INSTANCING: 'EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing'
```

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L494
author: bhouston

body: BTW Alban over at Sketchfab liked the extension when I mentioned it to him - finally all those models over at Sketchfab that have bumps can actually export that info: https://twitter.com/benhouston3d/status/1716640855455412305
author: bhouston

body:
@bhouston Understood! I have no objection to EXT_ extensions in general. Our policy has been to implement them on a case-by-case basis, if we feel they're good for our users. We do not support `EXT_lights_image_based` or `EXT_lights_ies` – and with reason.  If `EXT_materials_bump` benefits three.js and its users, we should support it. Unlike some engines, loading a bump map at runtime does not cost three.js anything.



Positive interest from Sketchfab is encouraging. 👍🏻
author: donmccurdy

body: @donmccurdy why don't we support the IES extension?  I figured it was just because we never got around to it.  We technically have an IES loader (which I provided the initial code for) as well as an IES light type.  Our code base aligns well with that extension.
author: bhouston

body: Also we could likely support the EXT_lights_image_based as well.   While I am not a complete fan of its dual cube map + SH approach, we could do it anyway.  It is a major limitation that we can not pass around HDR files in glTFs.
author: bhouston

body:
Perhaps we should support `EXT_lights_ies`. Mostly it's newer, nothing exports it, and I don't care to lead the charge on more complexity in GLTFLoader at this time.

I don't view `EXT_lights_image_based` as a production-ready approach to IBL. https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/1956 may be better. If we want to nudge things along I'd sooner ship an implementation we like under `THREE_*`, than promote the wrong `EXT_*` extension toward becoming the de facto standard.

But we can have a new issue for either, if that's of interest.
author: donmccurdy

body: @elalish Any thoughts about `EXT_materials_bump`?
author: mrdoob

body:
A few things: 
>here is the list of other EXT_ extensions that ThreeJS supports

These are actually `EXT` extensions that have since been [ratified](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/README.md#ratified-khronos-extensions-for-gltf-20), so more like KHR now.

>It is a major limitation that we can not pass around HDR files in glTFs.

I would disagree, especially considering how huge HDR files are (we really need a better image format). Packing the lighting into the file removes much of the point of PBR (separation of object from lighting), especially since it means you can't easily reuse the lighting for multiple models. For scenes where you want the lighting packaged with the model, a NeRF-style system would be much better anyway. I'm sure when that settles down a bit a format standard will be developed for that too.

>Any thoughts about EXT_materials_bump?

I've put my opinions [here](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/2339#discussion_r1369328371) and [here](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/2339#discussion_r1369329373). I'm concerned about artist workflow fragmentation mostly, but I don't have a very strong opinion. It would help to know a bit more about the state of the industry.
author: elalish

body:
I would like to have some additional (more general) information about bump and normal maps. 

I have often read that bump maps are considered as a predecessor of normal maps. And that means if a system supports normal maps, there is no reason for using bump maps since you can do the same with normal maps. Is that actually true? In other words: Is there a use case that can be implemented with bump maps but not with normal maps?

If not, I wonder why having both map types? Wouldn't it be more intuitive and simple to just have one solution that covers all use cases? Supporting `EXT_materials_bump` somewhat means breathing new live into something that felt obsolete, imo.
author: Mugen87

body:
> somewhat means breathing new live into something that felt obsolete

@Mugen87 if bump maps are obsolete then maybe we should remove bump map support from ThreeJS?
author: bhouston

body: I think being able to combine bumpMap and normalMap is powerful. Specially now that we support multi uvs and multi transforms.
author: mrdoob

body: Okay, let's give it a go 👌
